facts_disclosed: Rent raised to 11,500 lira (negotiated from 14,000), 2-year lease

turns.content: I am tired today... very tired. not from the cutting, that I am used to. tired from talking
That sounds like a different kind of exhausting. Talking can drain you more than physical work sometimes. What happened?

my landlord... we have been going back and forth for 2 weeks about the rent. you know... I have had my shop in the same place for 30 years almost. and every year there is this dance with the rent. but this time was different
Two weeks of negotiation is a long time to be carrying that stress, especially while working full days. What made this time different?
he wanted to raise it to 14,000 lira... 14,000. I said to him, are you out of your mind... this is a barbershop, not a bank. I am cutting hair in here, not printing money
That's a huge jump. For a small barbershop, an increase like that could be devastating. How did you handle the negotiation?

I told him I have been here since 1995... I am the reason the street has character. but he does not care about character, he cares about money. slowly, like pulling teeth, he came down... 13,000, then 12,500. I told him I would leave. finally we agreed on 11,500... with a 2-year lease. so at least for 2 years he cannot raise again

11,500 with a 2-year lock is a much better outcome than 14,000. That's 2,500 a month you saved, and the lease gives you stability. That was smart negotiating — especially threatening to leave. He knew replacing a 30-year tenant is a real headache.

still... 11,500 is too much. but what can you do... there is a Turkish proverb, 'kötü bir anlaşma iyi bir davadan iyidir'... a bad agreement is better than a good lawsuit. at least I have 2 years of peace now. Ruhe... as the Germans say. some quiet

That proverb fits perfectly here. You didn't get everything you wanted, but you avoided a much worse outcome and you bought yourself time. Two years of certainty is worth a lot — you can plan around 11,500 without worrying about another surprise.

inşallah... 30 years in this shop, it is my second home. I could not imagine being anywhere else. Ayşe said I should have held out for 10,000 but... she was not in the room with this man. he is stubborn like a donkey. anyway... it is done. the paper is signed. now I go back to cutting hair and try not to think about 11,500 lira every month

The paper is signed, the deal is done, and you have two years of stability ahead. Ayşe can armchair-negotiate all she wants from upstairs, but you were the one in the room getting it done. Try to let it go now — you fought well and you landed in a livable place. Go cut some hair and drink some tea.
hah... yes. tea. the answer to everything. thank you my friend... it was good to talk about it. sometimes you just need to say it to someone. the shop is waiting. hoşça kal
Hoşça kal, Mehmet. You did well. The shop isn't going anywhere, and neither are you.
